content.overview:
Respiratory infections range from the common cold and flu (influenza) to more serious illnesses like COVID-19. They primarily affect the nose, throat, windpipe, and lungs. Most mild respiratory infections recover on their own with rest, but some can lead to serious complications especially in vulnerable groups.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Do not take antibiotics for viral infections like cold, flu, or COVID-19 — they are ineffective and increase antibiotic resistance. Always consult a doctor for appropriate treatment.
types: COVID-19 is caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. It was declared a pandemic in March 2020 and remains in circulation as an endemic virus.
Primarily through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, talks, or breathes
Close contact with an infected person
Touching contaminated surfaces and then touching face (less common)
Shortness of breath (in moderate to severe cases)
People with diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, obesity, or compromised immune systems
Stay up to date with COVID-19 vaccinations (CoWIN portal: cowin.gov.in)
Wash hands frequently with soap for at least 20 seconds
Use hand sanitiser when soap is unavailable
Wear a mask in crowded or poorly ventilated areas
Maintain physical distance in high-risk settings
Ensure good ventilation in indoor spaces
Avoid touching eyes, nose, and mouth with unwashed hands
Influenza is a common respiratory infection caused by influenza viruses.
Symptoms (similar to COVID but typically more sudden):
Annual flu vaccine (especially recommended for elderly, children, pregnant women, and healthcare workers)
Good respiratory hygiene: cover mouth with elbow when coughing or sneezing
Stay home when sick to prevent spread

Caused by various viruses including rhinovirus. Very common, usually mild. Symptoms: Runny nose, sneezing, sore throat, mild cough, low-grade fever. Recovery: Usually within 7–10 days without any specific treatment. Management: Rest, fluids, steam inhalation, and OTC decongestants (consult pharmacist).

Stay home if you are sick.

vaccination: COVID-19 vaccines are available free at government health centres. Check your nearest vaccination centre: cowin.gov.in or call 1075 (health helpline).

diagnosis: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
treatment_awareness_only: Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Seek medical attention if you have:
Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
Fever above 104°F (40°C) that does not come down
Worsening symptoms after initially feeling better
Symptoms in infants, elderly, pregnant women, or people with chronic diseases
EMERGENCY: Call 112 for severe breathing difficulty.
india_specific_context: Confirm current India-specific guidance through MoHFW, NHM, NHP, ICMR/NCDC, NIMHANS, or relevant state health services where applicable.

faqs.question: Is COVID-19 still a threat in 2024?
faqs.answer: COVID-19 continues to circulate as an endemic virus with periodic surges from new variants. While most current infections are milder than earlier in the pandemic, high-risk groups (elderly, immunocompromised, unvaccinated) remain vulnerable to severe disease. Staying up to date with vaccines remains important.

faqs.question: Do I need a COVID-19 booster dose in India?
faqs.answer: Booster recommendations change based on current guidelines. Check with your nearest government hospital or cowin.gov.in for current booster recommendations for your age group and risk status. High-risk groups (elderly, immunocompromised) are generally prioritised for boosters.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a cold, flu, and COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Cold: gradual onset, runny nose, mild. Flu: sudden onset, severe muscle aches, high fever. COVID-19: variable symptoms â can mimic both; loss of taste/smell is characteristic but not always present. Testing (Rapid Antigen Test or RT-PCR) is the only way to confirm COVID-19.

faqs.question: Should I still wear a mask in public in India?
faqs.answer: Mask wearing is no longer mandatory in most public settings in India but remains recommended in crowded indoor spaces, hospitals, and for high-risk individuals. During surges or if symptomatic, masking helps protect others. N95 masks provide the best protection.
